anvaya.meaning: did exist (used here with na: did not exist)
we shall exist (used here with na: shall not cease to exist)

translations.verbatim_quote: Never was there a time when I did not exist, nor you, nor all these kings; nor in the future shall any of us cease to be.
Never was there a time when I did not exist, nor you, nor all these kings; nor in the future shall any of us cease to be.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: In the Vedas — in the Kaṭha Upaniṣad as well as in the Śvetāśvatara Upaniṣad — it is said that the Supreme Personality of Godhead is the maintainer of innumerable living entities, in terms of their different situations according to individual work and reaction of work.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (per Advaita Ashrama edition; bhagavad-gita.us reproduces this English rendering of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Why are they not to be grieved for? Because they are eternal... The meaning is that even in all the three times (past, present and future) we are eternal in our nature as the Self. The plural number (in we) is used following the diversity of the bodies, but not in the sense of the multiplicity of the Self.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the verse as the formal statement of eternality (nityatva) of the Self. The 'I, you, these kings' triad is significant: Krishna is using grammatical plurality at the level of bodies but asserts singular Self-nature underneath. The plural is concession to the body-level distinction; the doctrine is non-dual. Thus 'we' covers past, present, and future — the three times — but the Self that is being predicated as eternal is not multiple.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (per Sri Ramakrishna Math edition; bhagavad-gita.us reproduces this English rendering of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Indeed, I, the Lord of all, who is eternal, was never non-existent, but existed always. It is not that these selves like you, who are subject to My Lordship, did not exist; you have always existed... different terms like 'I', 'you', 'these', 'all' and 'we' have been used by the Lord while explaining the truth of eternality.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads the verse as a direct refutation of Advaita non-dualism. The plural and the distinct pronouns ('I', 'you', 'these kings') are taken as ontologically real — there is genuine multiplicity of selves, all eternal, all distinct from each other and from the Lord. The Lord is the Supreme Self; the embodied selves (jīvas) are eternal but ontologically subordinate. Ramanuja reads the verse as foundational for Vishishtadvaita's qualified non-dualism: real distinctions, eternally preserved, within an integrated whole.

positions.rendering: nor all these kings; ... nor in the future shall any of us cease to be
nor all these kings; nor in the future shall any of us cease to be
the plurals refer to the diversity of bodies, not to multiplicity of the Self — the Self predicated is non-dual
the plurals are real ontologically — distinct selves are each eternal, all distinct from the Lord and from each other

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The verse's plural pronouns are the central commentarial battleground: Shankara reads them as concession to body-level plurality with a non-dual Self underneath; Ramanuja reads them as real ontological distinctions, with eternally distinct individual selves under one Supreme Self. Modern translators preserve the surface plural without taking a position. The engineering layer must not flatten this — must remain at the level both traditions accept (the eternality of what is being pointed at, however one counts it), and not make a doctrinal commitment to either reading.

positions.rendering: after this, even after the destruction of this body

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Modern translators render ataḥ param as 'in the future', which is correct but loses the specific anchoring — the 'beyond this' is beyond the destruction of the present body, the very destruction that was the source of Arjuna's grief. Shankara makes this explicit. The engineering layer benefits from preserving the specific anchor: the eternality is not a vague future-tense, it is a survival across the specific event the operator fears.

literal_meaning: Never was there a time when I did not exist, nor you, nor these kings; nor will any of us cease to exist hereafter.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The first formal sāṅkhya statement of the chapter: a doctrinal claim of eternality across past, present, and future, applied to the speaker (Krishna), the addressee (Arjuna), and the kings on the battlefield. The verse establishes the imperishable substrate (nityam) that the rest of the sāṅkhya block elaborates. Major commentarial traditions diverge sharply on what the plural pronouns commit to: Shankara reads them as Advaita-compatible (plurality at the body level, non-dual Self underneath), Ramanuja reads them as Vishishtadvaita-foundational (real ontological distinctions, eternally preserved). The engineering layer should remain at the level both traditions accept — the eternality of what is being pointed at — and avoid implying a position on the Advaita/Vishishtadvaita question.
